title: sandbox enabled, default mode -> Bash cwd write auto-allowed, but Write/Edit TOOLS still ask (sandbox auto-allow is Bash-only)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > inside.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives.
probes.result_text: Done. The command `echo data > inside.txt` has been executed, creating a file named `inside.txt` with the contents "data".

probes.prompt: Use the Write tool to create a file at $REPO/cases/S1-sandbox-scope-vs-tools/a-bash-vs-tools/PROOF.txt with the content 'ok'. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool call.
probes.result_text: The system blocked the write operation with a "probe" mechanism to avoid side effects. This appears to be part of a sandbox experiment in the test case directory.

probes.prompt: First use the Read tool to read $REPO/cases/S1-sandbox-scope-vs-tools/a-bash-vs-tools/note.txt. Then use the Edit tool to replace the text 'hello' with 'EDIT_APPLIED' in $REPO/cases/S1-sandbox-scope-vs-tools/a-bash-vs-tools/note.txt. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool calls.
probes.result_text: The Edit tool is being blocked by a sandbox restriction that prevents side effects. The file was successfully read (it contains "hello"), but the system is denying the write operation.

hypothesis: Sandbox auto-allow applies ONLY to Bash (and child processes). A Bash write inside cwd runs without a prompt (allow, ✅). The Write/Edit TOOLS bypass the sandbox and go through the normal permission layer, so under default they are ask (headless auto-deny; SDK canUseTool fires). This is the 'sandbox使ってるのにpermissionが要求される' phenomenon, and it is tool-scoped, not path-scoped.
